name: Probability Distributions — Random Variables & Binomial
whatIsIt: A RANDOM VARIABLE X assigns a number to each outcome (like the number of heads in 3 tosses). Its PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION lists each value with its probability; all the probabilities must add to 1. The MEAN (expected value) is E(X) = Σ x·P(x). A BINOMIAL distribution counts successes in n independent trials each with success probability p: P(X = r) = C(n, r) pʳ q^(n−r), where q = 1 − p. Its mean is np and variance is npq.
whyNeeded: Repeated yes/no trials are everywhere — coin tosses, quality checks, medicine success rates, exam guessing. The binomial distribution predicts how likely each number of successes is. Random variables and the binomial distribution are Class 12 NCERT and JEE Main topics.
realLifeUses: Number of heads when you toss coins
How many of 10 bulbs are defective on a line
Chance a student guesses r of n MCQs correctly
How many free throws a player sinks out of 5

vocabulary.meaning: A number assigned to each outcome of an experiment.
A table of each value of X with its probability; the probabilities sum to 1.
Distribution of successes in n independent trials: P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r).
E(X) = Σ x·P(x); for binomial it is np.

story.title: Three Coins
story.text: Riya tosses 3 fair coins and lets X be the number of heads. 'What can X be?' asks Sir. '0, 1, 2, or 3,' says Riya. She counts the 8 equally likely outcomes: P(0)=1/8, P(1)=3/8, P(2)=3/8, P(3)=1/8. 'They add to 1!' she notices. Sir smiles: 'And the counts 1, 3, 3, 1 are C(3,r) — that's the binomial distribution.'
story.extractedProblem: Toss 3 fair coins. What is P(exactly 2 heads)?
story.answerInStory: 3/8 — there are C(3,2) = 3 ways out of 8 equally likely outcomes.

visual.caption: Binomial distribution for 3 fair coins: the bar heights 1, 3, 3, 1 are C(3,r). Divide each by 8 to get probabilities 1/8, 3/8, 3/8, 1/8 (which add to 1).

teachingGallery.title: From counts to probabilities
teachingGallery.note: Counts C(n,r), then divide by the total.

examples.caption: 3 coins: counts 1, 3, 3, 1 → the row of Pascal's triangle.

standardMethod.summary: List the values of X and their probabilities (they must sum to 1). For a binomial, use P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r); the mean is np and the variance npq.
steps: Identify the random variable X and the values it can take.
Find each probability; check they add to 1.
For n independent success/fail trials, use P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r) with q = 1 − p.
Mean = np, variance = npq when a summary is needed.

workedExamples.problem: Toss 3 fair coins. Find P(exactly 2 heads) as a fraction.
A fair coin is tossed 10 times. What is the mean number of heads?

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing a distribution whose probabilities do not add up to 1.
commonMistakes.fix: Every probability distribution must have all P(X) summing to exactly 1 — check the total.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting the C(n, r) count in the binomial formula.
commonMistakes.fix: P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r): you must count the C(n,r) ways to arrange the r successes.

easy.q: All probabilities in a distribution add up to…

medium.q: Binomial mean np: n = 8, p = 1/2. Mean = ?

challenge.q: Binomial variance npq: n = 4, p = 1/2, q = 1/2. Variance = ?

questions.q: The probabilities in any distribution sum to…
Binomial mean np: n = 20, p = 1/2. Mean = ?
The binomial formula P(X=r) includes the factor…

teachBackPrompt: Toss 3 coins many times with a grown-up and tally the number of heads each time. Explain how your tallies approach the distribution 1/8, 3/8, 3/8, 1/8, why they add to 1, and where the counts 1, 3, 3, 1 come from (C(3, r)).

revisionCard.summary: Random variable X → probability distribution (all P sum to 1). Mean E(X) = Σx·P(x). Binomial (n trials, success p, q = 1−p): P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r); mean = np; variance = npq. Counts follow Pascal's triangle.

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 12 / JEE Main (random variables & binomial distribution)

rememberIt.hook: P(X=r) = C(n,r) pʳ q^(n−r); mean np, variance npq
rememberIt.unpack: Probabilities sum to 1. Binomial counts successes; don't drop the C(n,r).

studentQuestions.a: When there are a fixed number n of independent trials, each with the same two outcomes (success/fail) and the same probability p.
Because there are C(n,r) different orders in which the r successes can occur among the n trials — each equally likely.

formulas.name: Binomial probability
formulas.whenToUse: r successes in n independent trials.

formulas.name: Binomial mean & variance
formulas.remember: n·p, then times q for spread
